segments.s460-g33: This is a simple nominal list with a past verb. درم داد is literally “(he) gave dirhams”: درم (direct object) precedes the past verb داد as is common in classical Persian narration. The following و + noun (و دینار و برگستوان) continue the list of direct objects; the verb داد governs the whole series.
segments.s460-g34: همان is a demonstrative meaning “the same/likewise,” referring back to what was given; it functions adverbially before the noun list. The nouns جوشن، تیغ، گرز are listed in normal Persian order (noun then coordinating و), and the adjective گران follows the noun it qualifies (postpositive adjective) to yield “heavy/valuable maces.”
segments.s460-g35: هرانکس که introduces a relative clause ‘whoever/whosoever who…’. بد کار is a noun phrase where بد (adjective) modifies کار (deed) to mean ‘bad deed’; دیده is a past participle ‘seen’/‘having been seen’ functioning adjectivally with کار (i.e. ‘who had the reputation/record of ill-deeds’). سری here is the noun ‘head’ (i.e. person/leader) used as the logical patient in the following clause.
segments.s460-g36: ببخشید is a past narrative form of بخشیدن (here ‘they/he pardoned’ or ‘bestowed’ depending on context) and takes the following prepositional phrase بر هر سری کشوری as its indirect/beneficiary phrase: بر ‘upon/on’, هر سری ‘each head/person’, کشوری qualifying سری as ‘of the realm’. The sentence omits the direct object (understood as pardon/gift) which is recovered from the context.
segments.s460-g37: یکی را is an indefinite object/topic marker construction (‘one/a certain person’ with را marking it). ز introduces the source “from the rebels” (ز + گردنکشان). مرز داد is the verb phrase: مرز (noun) followed by the past verb داد ‘gave’ — literally ‘gave a border/from the rebels’ (literal phrasing preserved; exact idiomatic meaning depends on context).
segments.s460-g38: سپه را is a topicalized/accusative-marked noun phrase ‘the army’ (را marking definite object or topic). همه چیز is the direct object ‘everything,’ and باارز (preceding the verb) qualifies it as ‘valuable/esteemed’; داد is the past verb ‘gave’. Word order: Topic (سپه را) + object (همه چیز باارز) + verb (داد).
segments.s460-g39: فرستاده آمد is a straightforward subject + past verb ‘a messenger came’. ز هر کشوری uses the poetic/prepositional ز for ‘from’ and هر کشوری (‘every country’) is the source adjunct; Persian word order places the adjunct after the verb here for narrative rhythm.
segments.s460-g40: This is a continuation of the source phrase: ز هر نامداری و هر مهتری = ‘from every renowned person and every chief’. The coordinating و links the two parallel noun phrases; each هر + noun is distributive ‘each/every’. The preposition ز governs the whole coordinated phrase.
segments.s460-g17: ز introduces a genitive/partitive list ('of' or 'from' in poetic use) that governs the nouns that follow. The final مراست is a fused form مرا + است ('is mine' / 'belongs to me'), so the whole clause reads literally 'Of the world, (the) sun and portion and covenant is mine.' Word order places the possessive predicate مراست at the end, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s460-g18: This is a parallel nominal list linked by و whose predicate is again the fused مراست ('is mine'). The nouns (بزرگی، شاهی، فرمان) are predicates in apposition to the previous list, reinforcing what belongs to the speaker. The repeated مراست at the end makes the copular possession explicit.
segments.s460-g19: دبیر خردمند را is a definite/accusative object marked by را (object marker). پیش خواند is a verbal phrase where پیش ('before; in the presence of') combines with خواند; here خواند is used in the sense 'to call/summon', so 'he called/summoned the wise secretary forward.' The verb is past tense, third person.
segments.s460-g20: ز هر در ('from every gate/quarter') is a circumstantial prepositional phrase; فراوان modifies سخنها and functions adverbially ('abundantly, many'), and براند is a past/present-poetic form of راندن used here as 'he sent forth / he uttered' (i.e., he put many words forth). The clause therefore means 'from every quarter he uttered abundant words.'
segments.s460-g21: Word order is marked/poetic: یکی نامه بنوشت فرخ دبیر reads as 'a certain letter wrote Farrokh the scribe' => natural reading: 'Farrokh the scribe wrote a certain letter.' یکی functions as 'a certain' (indefinite), نامه is the direct object, and بنوشت is an archaic/past verbal form meaning 'wrote.' The name and title follow the verb as the agent.
segments.s460-g22: ز here introduces the source/agentive phrase 'from/on behalf of' (poetic usage). دارای is an adjective/epithet ('possessing' / 'lordly') modifying داراب (a proper name), and بن followed by a name is a patronymic ('son of'); together the phrase names 'Darab, son of Ardashir' as the origin or party associated with the letter. Note: بن here functions as part of the patronymic, not the literal 'root' sense.
segments.s460-g23: بهر سو is a directional phrase ('to/whichever side'); که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('where/that which'), so بهر سو که ... reads 'to whichever side where ...'. بد شاه و خودکامه‌ای is a noun phrase: بد شاه 'an evil/bad king' and خودکامه‌ای ('a self-willed/tyrannical one') is an appositive adjective with the indefinite/adversative suffix -ای giving the sense 'a self-willed (person)'.
segments.s460-g24: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb meaning 'he caused/commanded/ordered' (here 'he sent/issued'). چون is the comparative/simile 'like/as', so چون خنجری نامه‌ای = 'a letter like a dagger.' نامه‌ای is indefinite ('a letter') and functions as the object of بفرمود; خنجری is adjectival ('dagger-like' or 'resembling a dagger').
segments.s460-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; دارا is the subject (here the king Dārā). به دل + noun places the feeling 'in the heart' (preposition before the noun). سوک is an archaic/poetic noun meaning a yearning or lament in this context, and داشت is the past verb 'had' expressing possession/feeling.
segments.s460-g2: This is the main clause: به خورشید is a prepositional/image phrase ('with/like the sun' or 'upon the sun' in poetic hyperbole), تاج مهی = 'a moon‑like crown' (تاج head noun, مهی postpositive adjective). برفراشت is a past tense compound verb meaning 'raised/erected' (بر‑فراشت).
segments.s460-g3: یکی مرد is an indefinite nominative 'a certain man'; a series of adjectives follows the noun (بد تیز و برنا و تند) joined by و. In classical Persian بد here functions as an emphatic/qualifying particle (not literal 'bad'), and the adjectives characterize the man (sharp/keen, youthful/vigorous, swift/impetuous).
segments.s460-g4: شده is a past participle/auxiliary 'having become' linking back to the preceding adjectives. با introduces the instrumental phrase 'with' (با زبان و دلش = 'with his tongue and his heart'); ش is the enclitic possessive 'his' attached to دل. تیغ is the object 'sword' and کند is the present/simple verb 'makes/turns into' — here idiomatically 'uses/turns into a sword'.
segments.s460-g5: چو بنشست signals 'when he sat down' (narrative past); برگاه is the locative 'on the royal court/platform'. گفت here functions as the past tense verb 'he said' introducing direct address; ای + سران is the vocative 'O leaders'.
segments.s460-g6: This is the vocative continuation: سرافراز is an adjectival epithet 'honored/exalted' modifying the following nouns. گردان here denotes a troop/division (addressed collectively), و links items, and کنداوران is a plural noun addressed in the same vocative line. Word order is typical for classical Persian vocative epithets followed by the nouns they modify.
segments.s460-g7: سری را is the definite object (را marks the object) 'a head'; نخواهم is the first‑person negative 'I do not/will not want'. که + افتد is a relative/subordinate clause 'that falls'; افتد is classical present/subjunctive form meaning 'falls' or 'should fall', and به چاه gives the destination/source 'into the pit/well'.
segments.s460-g8: نه introduces the negative continuation 'nor'; از چاه is the source 'from the pit'; خوانم is first‑person 'I call/summon' (here 'I will not summon'); سوی تخت و گاه is the destination phrase 'toward the throne and the court/seat' (سوی = toward, تخت = throne, گاه = court/time/seat). Word order places source before the verb and destination after it, typical in classical diction.
segments.s460-g9: کسی is an indefinite subject ('anyone'). کو here is an archaic/poetic relative particle linking the subject to the clause ('who'); it does not mean 'where' in this use. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/against', and فرمان من is the noun + possessor ('my command'). بگذرد is third-person present/subjunctive-style verb meaning 'passes beyond / transgresses' (here: 'disobeys').
segments.s460-g10: سرش را is 'his head' + object marker (سر + enclitic possessive ش + را). همی is an archaic/emphatic particle (here reinforcing the negative: 'indeed/will not'). تن به سر is a collocation literally 'body for head' (i.e. treating head and body as one). نشمرد is the negative present/preterital form of شمردن ('to count/consider') with ن-; here it means 'will not consider' or 'does not reckon'.
segments.s460-g11: وگر is a conditional 'and if' (classical Persian). هیچ تاب literally 'any strength/endurance' — هیچ used here as an indefinite ('any/ever'). تاب means 'endurance/strength'. اندر آرد is a verb phrase ('brings/has within') with اندر = 'in/within' and آرد = 'brings/puts', so the whole phrase means 'if he puts/has any endurance in his heart.'
segments.s460-g12: به شمشیر uses به instrumentally ('by/with a sword'). باشم is first-person singular present/subjunctive/hortative of بودن ('I am / I will be') used here as a resolute statement 'I shall be / I am'. ورا is 'to him' (vora = ve + ra-like clitic, here 'toward him'). دلگسل is an adjective 'heart‑rending' used predicatively to describe the speaker's role toward that person.
segments.s460-g13: جز از ما 'except from us' (i.e. 'except for any of ours'). هرانکس که is a classical relative expression 'whoever (who)'. دارند is the present plural 'they have/possess' used generically with هرانکس ('whoever has'); گنج is the noun 'treasure'. The clause means 'except those among us who possess treasure'.
segments.s460-g14: نخواهم is the first‑person negative of خواستن here ('I do not want / I will not permit'). کسی is the indefinite object 'anyone'. شاددل is an adjective 'cheerful / glad' describing that anyone. ما is 'us/our' and به رنج is a prepositional phrase 'at/over (our) suffering' — together: 'I do not want anyone to be glad at our suffering.' The verb (نخواهم) governs the clause that follows.
segments.s460-g15: نخواهم که is a classical construction 'I do not want that...'. باشد is the subjunctive/third‑person form of بودن ('there should be / that there be'). م + را appear as the split object pronoun 'مرا' (me/to me) — here marking the person for whom the guide would exist. رهنمای is the noun 'guide/adviser'. The whole phrase means 'I do not want anyone to be my guide' or literally 'I do not want that there should be a guide for me.'
segments.s460-g16: منم is the emphatic copula-like 'I am' (من + enclitic م), introducing a predicate. رهنمای is predicate noun 'guide'. و links the two predicates, and the second منم + دلگشای repeats the emphasis: 'I myself am the guide and I myself am the heart‑easer/comforter.' This is a classic emphatic doubleness common in epic diction.
segments.s460-g25: که introduces a relative/conditional clause here ('who/that'). هرکو is an indefinite relative meaning 'whoever'. ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from' and takes the following nouns رای and فرمان as sources ('from my counsel and from my command'). من is a postposed possessor ('my'); Persian commonly puts the possessor after the possessed noun in such phrases.
segments.s460-g26: Two verbs with the prefixed ب- appear in sequence. بپیچد (ب + پیچد) is the present/subjunctive/optative form of پیچیدن used here as 'turn away/deviate' in the protasis ('whoever turns away...'). ببیند is another present-form verb 'he will see' or 'let him see' giving the outcome; Persian often chains verbs like this to state conditional result. سرافشان is a descriptive compound 'سر + افشان' meaning 'with hair dishevelled' (adjectival phrase); من is the clitic/word 'me' referring back to the speaker.
segments.s460-g27: همه گوش is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'everyone' or 'all ears' (i.e., give attention). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/wholly' modifying the verb. به فرمان is a prepositional phrase 'to the command' (به + noun). نهید is the present/subjunctive/imperative form of نهاد (نهادن) 'put/place' used here as an address to listeners: 'set (your ears) to the command' = 'give full attention to the order.' Note Persian word order: modifiers follow or precede flexibly, and the verb concludes the clause.
segments.s460-g28: The repeated اگر marks conditional or alternative possibilities: 'if' / 'whether'. جان is 'life' (i.e., to take someone's life or to spare/give life). ستانید is the present/subjunctive/imperative form of ستاندن 'to take/deprive' (here 'you take [a life]'). دهید is the present/subjunctive/imperative of دادن 'to give' (here 'you give/spare'). The structure presents two opposing actions as the matters to heed.
segments.s460-g29: سر here means 'top/head' or 'chief part' and forms a possessive construction with گنجهای پدر ('the treasures of the father') — together 'the chief vaults/treasuries of his father'. برگشاد is a past participle/verb meaning 'opened' (بر + گشاد, classical perfective), placed after the noun phrase as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s460-g30: سپه را marks 'the army' as the definite object (را). همه qualifies it as 'the whole army.' خواند is here 'he called/summoned' (one of its primary meanings). The conjunction و links to روزی بداد: روزی in context means 'pay/provisions/sustenance' and بداد is the classical perfective form 'he gave' (be‑ + داد); together 'and he gave them their pay/provisions.' Note: روزی and بداد are somewhat idiomatic here (giving wages/rations) and the lexical choices in the provided sense lists do not exactly match, so their contextual meanings are described.
segments.s460-g31: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. چار here most likely refers to 'the four (treasure-chests/quarters)' — the numeral/collective sense 'four'. اندرآمد is used in the sense of 'income/what came forth' or 'came in/out' (classical usage meaning revenue or 'what was produced'), and درم is the dirham coin. تا بهشت is a hyperbolic phrase ('up to Paradise') describing abundance. The whole phrase says that dirhams poured forth from the four (sources) in great abundance.
segments.s460-g32: یکی را ... و یکی را ... is a distributive pattern 'one/one of them ... and another ...' with را marking each object. The surface بجام should be read as به جام ('into a cup') in this context (orthographic contraction common in poetry), though the provided lemma list did not offer that sense; here it functions as a prepositional phrase 'into the cup'. به تشت is 'into the basin.' The clause describes how he divided the coins: some into a cup, others into a basin.
segments.s460-g41: This is a coordinated list introduced by the preposition ز (poetic 'from') repeated for emphasis before each item. Word order is nominal: each place or title follows ز; فغفور چین is a title + place construction (the faghfur of China). There is no finite verb in this segment — it functions as one item in the larger enumerative clause.
segments.s460-g42: Continues the same enumerative pattern: ز introduces 'from Rome' and then again 'from every country' (ز هر کشوری). همچنین ('likewise'/'also') caps the list. هر کشوری is a generic expression 'every/each country.'
segments.s460-g43: همه پاک is a compact phrase: همه 'all/everyone' modified by پاک used adverbially ('entirely' / 'all together'). The preposition با introduces a list of accompanying things (با هدیه و باژ و ساو), joined with و. This is a circumstantial phrase describing how they came (with gifts, with shouts/cry, and with Saveh).
segments.s460-g44: This line uses negation in a paired (neither... nor...) structure. نه پی بود negates 'there was no following/pursuit'; با او کسی را is an inverted order meaning 'no one was with him' (کسی را with object marker emphasizes the person), and the second نه negates تاو ('that radiance/glow'). بود is the past copula. The clause is elliptic/poetic in word order — the negations bracket the predicates.
segments.s460-g45: یکی functions as an indefinite subject 'a certain one / someone.' شارستان is the (direct) object 'town/city' and کرد is the past tense verb 'made/built.' The following noun phrase نوشاد نام is an object-complement construction: classical Persian often places the given name after the verb and uses نام ('name') to indicate the name assigned — i.e., 'he built a town and named it Nushad.'
segments.s460-g46: به اهواز is a prepositional phrase marking direction/destination 'to Ahvaz.' گشتند is a plural past verb ('they went/turned/went off'). زو is the contracted form of ز + او meaning 'because of him / from him,' and shadkam (شادکام) is a predicate/adjective 'fortunate, joyful' linked to the subject by the preceding verb; the causal phrase (زو) explains why they were shadkam.
segments.s460-g47: کسی را که introduces an object + relative clause: 'anyone who...' درویش بد داد is a terse relative clause meaning 'who did ill to a dervish' (داد here as a past verb 'gave/did' in the sense 'inflicted/treated'). The final داد is the main verb 'gave' (here best understood as 'inflicted/assigned' a punishment or 'dealt with'); classical Persian can have repeated forms of داد with different senses in close succession. Overall the clause means he took action against anyone who harmed a dervish.
segments.s460-g48: به خواهندگان is the indirect-object phrase 'to the petitioners/claimants.' گنج و بنیاد are the direct objects 'treasure and foundation/endowment' (بنیاد here in the sense of an endowment or endowed foundation). The final داد is the past tense verb 'gave.' The segment states a benefaction: he granted treasure and endowments to those who asked.

sentences.s460-s17: He gave dirhams and dinars and a bargestuvān; likewise (he gave) the same cuirasses, swords, and heavy maces.
sentences.s460-s18: Whoever had committed bad deeds he pardoned; he bestowed (gifts) upon each head of a realm.
sentences.s460-s19: He gave one (a certain man) from the rebels a frontier / border; he gave the army everything valuable.
sentences.s460-s20: A messenger came from every country, from every notable and every chief.
sentences.s460-s9: Of the world — its sun, its portion, and its covenant — are mine; greatness, kingship, and command are mine.
sentences.s460-s10: He summoned the wise secretary before him, and from every gate spoke abundant words.
sentences.s460-s11: Farrokh the scribe wrote a certain letter from Darab the lord, son of Ardashir.
sentences.s460-s12: To every place where there was an evil, self-willed king, he ordered a letter like a dagger.
sentences.s460-s1: When Dārā had in his heart a longing for Dārāb, he raised a brilliant, moon‑like crown like the sun.
sentences.s460-s2: A certain man, fierce, keen and swift, having become so, with his tongue and heart turned them into a sword.
sentences.s460-s3: When he sat on the royal court, he said, 'O exalted chiefs of the host and excavators.'
sentences.s460-s4: I will not want a head that falls into a pit, nor will I summon (one) from the pit to the throne and court.
sentences.s460-s5: Anyone who disobeys my command — I will not count his head and body as one.
sentences.s460-s6: And if he has any strength in his heart, I will be a heart‑rending sword to him.
sentences.s460-s7: Except for any of us who possess treasure, I do not wish anyone to be glad at our suffering.
sentences.s460-s8: I do not want there to be (a) guide for me; I am the guide, and I am the comforter.
sentences.s460-s13: Whoever turns away from my counsel and my command will see me with my hair dishevelled.
sentences.s460-s14: Everyone, lend your whole ear to the command; whether you take life or grant it.
sentences.s460-s15: He opened the chief vaults of his father's treasures, summoned the whole army, and distributed pay.
sentences.s460-s16: From the four (treasuries) came dirhams in abundance; some he placed in a cup and some in a basin.
sentences.s460-s21: From India, from the khagan, from the faghfur of China, and likewise from Rome and every country.
sentences.s460-s22: All — entirely — with gifts, cries and Saveh; no one followed him, nor had that radiance.
sentences.s460-s23: A certain man built a town and named it Nushad; they went to Ahvaz and — because of him — were fortunate.
sentences.s460-s24: He punished anyone who did ill to a dervish; to petitioners he gave treasure and endowments.
